data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Northwestern University’s Swift Hall at 2021 Sheridan Rd. in Evanston, Thursday, March 26, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lisa Littman, one of Bailey’s co-investigators, serves as president of the Gender Dysphoria Institute, where Bailey also serves as treasurer. In its <a class="Link" href="https://www.gdinstitute.org/position-statement" target="_blank" ><u>position statement</u></a>, the organization aims to push back on the silencing of “science and proper research” into gender dysphoria that would otherwise be labeled transphobic.</p><p>In a 2018 study published in the journal PLOS One, Littman surveyed parents from “skeptic” sites to develop her theory of “rapid onset gender dysphoria,” or ROGD. It attempted to explain some trans youth as having picked up their identity from their peers.</p><p>The survey was later republished with a <a class="Link" href="https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0214157" target="_blank" ><u>correction</u></a> that said no youth or clinicians were surveyed and that parents’ reports on their children may not be accurate, and thus “does not validate the phenomenon.”</p><p>In an emailed statement, Littman said the evidence for ROGD was “early but growing.”</p><p>Leiszle Rae Lapping-Carr, a practicing psychologist at Northwestern Medicine and professor at Northwestern, said ROGD has “very little empirical support” and that many studies on it have been criticized for relying solely on parental reports. She said kids may not always share their feelings with parents depending on their perceived support.</p><p>“As [dysphoria] develops during puberty, it may seem sudden or rapid from the outside, but for the people experiencing it, it’s a natural progression,” Lapping-Carr said. “But [ROGD] has still been taken up to provide evidence for reduction in access to health care for trans folks.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/13/illinois-joins-suit-after-feds-threaten-billions-in-funding-over-trump-order-targeting-trans-people-again" >Illinois joins suit after feds threaten billions in funding over Trump order targeting trans people — again</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/24/illinois-joins-suit-trump-administrations-attempted-nationwide-ban-on-gender-affirming-care-for-youth" >Illinois joins lawsuit against White House’s attempted national ban on youth gender-affirming care</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But ROGD continues to be of key interest to the researchers. In 2023, Bailey <a class="Link" href="https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10508-023-02576-9" target="_blank" >published a paper</a> about ROGD in the journal Archives of Sexual Behavior<i> </i>based on data he said he received from a member of <a class="Link" href="http://parentsofrogdkids.com" target="_blank" ><u>ParentsofROGDKids.com</u></a>, which describes being trans as an “epidemic” among youth.</p><p>The journal retracted it less than 3 months later, with the editor-in-chief, Kenneth Zucker, and the publisher noting that the study's authors failed to get consent from survey subjects to share their information. They also noted that the authors didn't have approval from an Institutional Review Board, which monitors the ethics of human subject research. Bailey said in an interview with the Sun-Times that he thought those were "bogus reasons" for the retraction.</p><p>Zucker, also a co-researcher on AYAGDOS, weathered <a class="Link" href="https://www.medpagetoday.com/special-reports/exclusives/104685" target="_blank" >calls for his resignation</a> in the wake of the study's publication. He had previously seen his youth practice close in 2015 following accusations it was conducting a form of conversion therapy. The Toronto-based Centre for Addiction and Mental Health later <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/toronto/former-camh-psychologist-dr-kenneth-zucker-defends-his-work-1.4856371" target="_blank" ><u>settled with him in court</u></a>. He argued that at the time he aimed to “reduce gender dysphoria” by reinforcing dysphoric youths’ assigned gender at birth. He did not respond to multiple requests for comment.</p><p>And though AYAGDOS doesn’t mention ROGD by name, the study asks if an increase in trans children could “reflect a new type of gender dysphoria caused by peers influencing each other to adopt transgender identities.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1054139X23000708?casa_token=v6W4hP32npsAAAAA:PSHdz_edxHFQNZQaAbWTdDCAmzTCXFSckIWXrPvcRgZ_cu8lYkp46F05fN-hxrjw2pmJ5g" target="_blank" ><u>Other</u></a> <a class="Link" href="https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022347621010854" target="_blank" ><u>studies</u></a> have also <a class="Link" href="https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/evidence-undermines-rapid-onset-gender-dysphoria-claims/" target="_blank" ><u>discredited the theory</u></a>. A group of 62 major health care organizations, including the American Psychological Association and Howard Brown Health, <a class="Link" href="https://www.caaps.co/rogd-statement" target="_blank" ><u>signed a letter</u></a> in 2021 supporting "eliminating the use of ROGD … given the lack of empirical support for its existence and its likelihood of contributing to harm.” It said many of the hundreds of anti-trans bills filed that year were “predicated on the unsupported claims advanced by ROGD.”</p><p>Because of their past research, the researchers’ efforts have struggled, even as other, similar research is moving forward.</p><p>Shaun said "no amount of money" would offset the cost of participating in AYAGDOS after looking into the researchers’ history. He expects they will “replicate the issues they had with their past research.”</p><p>“You’re not going to get parents who are nuanced and understanding [to participate],” he said. “You’re going to get people who are reactionary.”</p><p>At the same time, he said his teen daughter has participated in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.jahonline.org/article/S1054-139X(24)00566-4/abstract" target="_blank" >similar five-year study</a> on the well-being of trans kids through her healthcare provider since she started receiving gender affirming care nearly four years ago.</p><p>Her care team connected her with researchers conducting that study before she filed her first prescription. While it was his daughter’s choice to participate, he said it comforted him that her providers made the introduction.</p><p>His daughter also gets a $100 gift card for each nearly three-hour long survey she completes, which he said asks questions about her mental and emotional wellbeing.</p><h2>‘I don’t know what I would change’</h2><p>Bailey denied having an ideological motive and also denied taking sides in what he called the “culture war.”</p><p>“The project is better with my participation,” Bailey said in response to concerns of his involvement with the ongoing study. “I can’t go back in time, and if I did, I don’t know what I would change.”</p><p>He also said that, though they haven’t turned down any money, he and his co-researchers would not accept “ideological pre-conditioning for funding.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">From Arthur Brooks in The Free Press, reminding gender critical intellectuals (transgender activists are beyond reminding): <a href="https://t.co/BaK1vB1O5L">pic.twitter.com/BaK1vB1O5L</a></p>— Michael Bailey (@profjmb) <a href="https://twitter.com/profjmb/status/2013589318917353792?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">January 20, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Still, he said he’s concerned “there’s been too much medical transition with too few safeguards.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://x.com/profjmb/status/2013926760190980109" target="_blank" >In a January post on X</a>, Bailey called himself a “scholarly ally” of the “gender-critical” movement, or those who believe sex assigned at birth is immutable. In 2024, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/profjmb/status/1874070996719984945?s=20" target="_blank" >he wrote</a>, “Of course we’d prefer our sons to have normal sexuality.” That was months after he was <a class="Link" href="https://missouriindependent.com/2024/10/02/credibility-of-states-expert-witnesses-questioned-in-missouri-transgender-health-care-trial/" target="_blank" ><u>called as an expert witness by the state of Missouri</u></a> in its defense of a law banning gender-affirming care for trans youths.</p><p>A bill filed by Illinois state Sen. Andrew Chesney last year and again this month that <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=2474&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&SessionID=114&GA=104" target="_blank" ><u>would outlaw gender-affirming care for minors</u></a> quoted Zucker and cited Bailey’s retracted study and Littman’s corrected publication on ROGD. Arkansas also used Littman’s paper to <a class="Link" href="https://arkansasadvocate.com/2022/11/29/judge-attorneys-scrutinize-witness-expertise-in-trial-on-arkansas-transgender-law/" target="_blank" ><u>defend its ban on gender-affirming care</u></a> for trans youths. It remains in committee.</p><p>And Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. said the American Medical Association and the American Academy of Pediatrics<b> </b>had “peddled a lie” in a <a class="Link" href="https://opa.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/2025-11/gender-dysphoria-report.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>November HHS report</u></a> that relied on all three AYAGDOS researchers. The report also said dysphoria is “not pathological and does not require treatment.”</p><p>An <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/24/illinois-joins-suit-trump-administrations-attempted-nationwide-ban-on-gender-affirming-care-for-youth" target="_blank" ><u>edict from Kennedy</u></a> based on that report, which had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/09/feds-agree-to-halt-proposed-national-youth-gender-care-ban-while-court-weighs-motion" target="_blank" ><u>threatened to cut off federal Medicaid and Medicare funding</u></a> from hospitals that provide the care, was <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/transgender-health-care-lawsuit-ruling-robert-kennedy-25adf96f745c5364c2ebf8c3f27cab71" target="_blank" ><u>struck down by a judge</u></a> earlier this month.</p><p>“We will find what we find,” Bailey said regarding possible future legislation. “And if somebody thinks our results are relevant to some proposed policy, if we can inform that, we will do it.”</p><p>That's what worries Lascano, who said the new study could be used to support such policies. She hopes Northwestern revamps its review process, saying university officials should have already intervened “to prevent harm.”</p><p>“The fact this was approved shows there is something lacking in that team at Northwestern,” Lascano said.</p><p>Shaun, the parent, said he was angry seeing people “scoring political points on behalf of my kid’s life.”</p><p>“There’s very little they could do to improve their study if they start with thinking their study subjects shouldn't exist,” he said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/09/feds-agree-to-halt-proposed-national-youth-gender-care-ban-while-court-weighs-motion" >Feds agree to halt proposed national youth gender care ban while court weighs motion</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/11/21/trans-day-remembrance-lgbtq" >On Trans Day of Remembrance, community reflects on those lost: ‘We’re gonna be here forever’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

entries.title: Review: FKA Twigs blends performance art and concert in emotional show at Wintrust Arena
entries.summary: <p>From the elite athleticism on display to the bold and revealing outfits confidently worn by her dancers, FKA Twigs’ current “Body High” tour is aptly named. But it goes beyond the physical form for the British singer-songwriter, who has endured the highest of highs and the lowest of lows in her 15-year career.</p><p>“Over the past couple of years, I found myself so ‘body low,’ and it’s taken me a lot of time and healing to feel ‘body high,’ and I wanted to create something that … could inspire us, me, everyone on stage, all of you just to fulfill our dreams,” she told the audience at Wintrust Arena Thursday night.</p><p>The body is a major theme of her show. Part of the tour tells a story of how she’s been recovering from the pain her body endured in more ways than one. In 2017, she underwent surgery to remove six large fibroid tumors from her uterus. She also alleged in a lawsuit filed in 2020 that former partner Shia LaBeouf abused her physically, emotionally and mentally during their relationship in 2018-19. The two settled the suit in 2025, but Twigs is still embroiled in a bitter legal battle with LaBeouf, accusing him of trying to silence her with a nondisclosure agreement, according to a new suit filed last week. Amid the turmoil, she also won her first <a class="Link" href="https://www.grammy.com/series-or-collection/2026-grammys-68th-annual-grammy-awards-videos/fka-twigs-eusexua-wins-best-dance-electronic-music-album-2026-grammys" target="_blank" ><u>Grammy</u></a> this year for her album “Eusexua,”<i> </i>and she’s been selling arenas like Madison Square Garden on her current tour.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jason Grohoske</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Chicago was one of two cities on her brief “Eusexua” tour last year, and the show, while compelling, was marred by production mishaps, not to mention derailed by visa issues that required rescheduling her North American dates. “Body High” took the highlights from that tour — Twigs’ deft performance on the pole, compelling stage performances and intricate and sensual choreography by Mike Tyus and Luca Renzi — and elevated it.</p><p>From the moment she arrived on stage Thursday night, lying on a bed-like platform covered in white sheets as she sang “Mirrored Heart,” Twigs firmly showed last year’s tour issues were behind her. Throughout the course of five acts, Twigs brought the audience to various worlds through her music and <a class="Link" href="https://news.wttw.com/2025/06/30/inside-chicago-s-queer-ballroom-scene-little-slice-fantasy" target="_blank" ><u>ballroom</u></a>-influenced set design, an ode to the culture, with an entertaining host leading the way.</p><p>While the tour is in support of her 2025 offering, “Eusexua Afterglow,”<i> </i>the evening’s set list was a two-hour multigenre celebration, honoring her hits, sleepers and deep cuts from just about every project she’s released. The steamy “Figure 8,” from the 2015 EP “M3LL155X,” was a treat she pulled out early into the show. During the hip-hop-inspired portion, she even performed her contributions to 645AR’s “Sum Bout You,” which was a pleasant surprise.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1c0000" name="image-1c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fcf6df9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0c3c41/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d984273/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="032626 Wintrust Arena - FKA twigs (22).jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d984273/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7568511/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d984273/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5488x3080+0+290/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fc7%2Fbcea58164222895e955f05accbb4%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-22.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>FKA twigs performs Thursday at Wintrust Arena as part of her Body High tour.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jason Grohoske</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While the Chicago stop didn’t have as much guest firepower as other shows on the tour, the stage presence of Twigs’ longtime dancer ensemble, many of whom she grew up with in London, more than made up for it. Dancer/teacher/choreographer James Vu Anh Pham shared blistering chemistry with Twigs on stage, while he also played clarinet and later the keys. The “Sticky” duet was a touching moment between her and another dancer who beautifully sang with her and played guitar.</p><p>Twigs’ soaring performance on the poles — one on the far left of the stage with the other one on the opposite side — showcased the decades of dance training she received since she was a child. What's often underestimated is the physical strength, stamina and precision it takes to move around the poles safely, and twice during the show she masterfully demonstrated her dexterity on the towering poles, twisting and twirling in sync to the music.</p><p>Draped in feathers, Twigs reemerged one final time with her dancers to close out the show with “Cellophane,” from her 2019 album “Magdalene.” As artificial snow came down behind her, she delivered one of her most emotional performances of the song. The room was so quiet, you could hear a phone drop.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a00000" name="image-a00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28b7021/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/abc4ef7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc8fb9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="032626 Wintrust Arena - FKA twigs (138).jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc8fb9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f3b1bde/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc8fb9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5359x3008+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F25%2Ff6dfcc0c4e5a97f6a17268e202d8%2F032626-wintrust-arena-fka-twigs-138.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>FKA twigs performs Thursday at Wintrust Arena as part of her Body High tour.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jason Grohoske</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“But I … just want to feel you’re there,” she tearfully whispered in a heart-wrenching, poignant moment. As the crowd silently wept alongside her, they were right there with her.</p><p>The “Body Tour” is a grand celebration of all her hard work and accomplishments amid the hardships she overcame to get to this point. For her, the dancers and crew she’s worked with for years, her tour showcases her as an artist reclaiming her voice and her body.</p><h3>FKA Twigs set list March 26, 2026 at Wintrust Arena</h3><p>Mirrored Heart<br>Meta Angel<br>Figure 8<br>Drums of Death<br>Hard<br>Lights On<br>Papi Bones<br>Video Girl<br>Sushi<br>Eusexua<br>Love Crimes<br>Room of Fools<br>Sticky<br>Stereo Boy<br>Water Me<br>Home With You<br>Fallen Alien<br>Mary Magdalene<br>Thousand Eyes<br>Two Weeks<br>Striptease<br>Cellophane</p>

entries.title: Three people shot, one fatally, at party in Will County
entries.summary: <p>One person was killed and two others injured in a shooting at a family party in far south suburban Crete early Sunday, according to the Will County sheriff's office.</p><p>The sheriff's office received several calls about a fight and possible shooting around 1 a.m. Sunday near 27000 S. State Line Road. Deputies arrived to find a large crowd and three shooting victims, who were taken to nearby hospitals, according to the sheriff's office.</p><p>One person later died at a hospital in Olympia Fields. They have yet to be identified.</p><p>The sheriff's office said the shooting appeared to be an isolated incident of domestic violence.</p><p>The shooting comes less than a week after a triple homicide in unincorporated Crete. Will County sheriff's deputies were called to conduct a wellness check in the 3400 block of East Norway Trail around 2 a.m. Monday and found three people fatally shot. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/indiana-woman-charged-in-crete-triple-homicide" target="_blank" >An Indiana woman was arrested and charged</a> with nine counts of first-degree murder. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Blackhawks rookie Artyom Levshunov sidelined with hand fracture
entries.summary: <p>NEWARK, N.J. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> rookie defenseman Artyom Levshunov's struggles in the last two games make more sense now.</p><p>The Hawks discovered Levshunov had been playing through a "small fracture" in his left hand that he suffered Tuesday against the Islanders, coach Jeff Blashill said Sunday morning.</p><p>"He's a tough kid," Blashill added.</p><p>Levshunov is now sidelined with an indefinite timetable pending further imaging, which should be done Monday when the Hawks return to Chicago. </p><p>It's very possible, if not likely, his season is now over, considering the Hawks have just nine games left (including Sunday against the Devils).</p><p>The Hawks are coming off back-to-back hideous losses against the Flyers and Rangers. During Levshunov's ice time in those two games, those opponents collectively outscored the Hawks 6-1 and outshot them 26-14.</p><p>It has been an up-and-down first full NHL season for the 20-year-old Belarusian, who peaked around New Year's, bottomed out before the Olympic break and had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/16/blackhawks-artyom-levshunov-defense-improved-moritz-seider-jeff-blashill" target="_blank" >relatively solid since the break</a> until this setback.</p><p>He has two goals and 22 assists (second among rookie defensemen) in 68 games, but he also has a minus-41 rating. That's heavily influenced by the fact he's played sizable minutes (19:35 per game) against opponents' top lines on one of the league's worst teams, but it's ugly nonetheless.</p><p>"I’m really excited about the way Arty has trended," general manager Kyle Davidson said earlier this month. "Certainly when you look at where he was a couple years ago to where he was a year [ago to now], I think it’s constant improvement. Maybe not constant upward trajectory, but in general, the arrow is definitely pointed up. We’re really excited about that. We think the sky is the limit for this kid."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/16/blackhawks-artyom-levshunov-defense-improved-moritz-seider-jeff-blashill" >How Artyom Levshunov has improved his defensive play along with the Blackhawks</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Kevin Korchinski was called up to take Levshunov's spot, and he will make his fifth NHL appearance of the season on the third pair alongside Ethan Del Mastro against the Devils.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Anton Frondell will play center tonight for the Blackhawks.<br><br>Blashill: “He hasn’t played center all year, so it’s probably not the easiest position to put him in — I recognize that. But he’s played center a lot in his life, and I think he’s got traits to be a really good 200-foot…</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2038287882461294951?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 29, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>

entries.title: West Sub's other owner vows to reopen suburban Oak Park hospital
entries.summary: <p>The other owner of West Suburban Medical Center says he is working on getting the hospital back open after it was abruptly closed by his business partner last week.</p><p>Rathnaker Reddy, who owns the West Suburban property in Oak Park and is a minority partner in the company that owns the hospital, Resilience Healthcare, said he was "deeply concerned" the hospital stopped patient services last week.</p><p>"I want to be clear that I have not been involved in the hospital's clinical operations or day-to-day management," Reddy said in a statement. "However, I have grown increasingly concerned about the trajectory of hospital operations, which has led to a reduction in services and, now, the ... closure of this critical community asset."</p><p>Manoj Prasad, the CEO and majority owner of Resilience Healthcare, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" >announced last week</a> he was temporarily closing West Suburban's hospital, emergency room and outpatient clinics. Prasad blamed the a new electronic medical record system for failing to bill for the hospital’s work for the last year. That has forced to the hospital to operate on a fraction of its usual revenue, he said.</p><p>In response, Reddy said in the statement he reached out to Insight Hospital & Medical Center to see if they can "play a constructive role in stabilizing operations at West Suburban." That company purchased Mercy Hospital and Medical Center in Bronzeville after it <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2021/10/25/inside-the-fight-to-save-chicagos-historic-mercy-hospital" target="_blank" >nearly closed in 2020</a>.</p><p>Atif Bawahab, CEO of Insight, said in the same statement: “Insight is able to support preserving healthcare services at West Suburban Hospital, similar to the role we played in maintaining Mercy Hospital. Our mission is to step into complex situations and maintain continuity of care for the sake of the community.”</p><p>The closure of West Suburban comes seven months after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" target="_blank" >Resilience Healthcare shuttered its other facility</a>, Weiss Memorial Hospital in Chicago’s Uptown neighborhood. Like Weiss, West Suburban serves a high percentage of patients who rely on Medicaid health insurance for people with low incomes or Medicare for seniors, according to the most recent state data.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/6/8/23160414/state-board-approves-sale-of-weiss-memorial-hospital-west-suburban-medical-center" target="_blank" >Prasad and Reddy purchased</a> West Suburban and Weiss in 2022.</p><p>Community members, hospital employees, and local and state officials said even temporarily closing West Suburban is devastating for Chicago's West Side and west suburban communities.</p><p>Reddy said he is working with Oak Park officials and state leaders, and "we remain open to engaging other qualified hospital systems, healthcare providers, and mission-aligned organizations that may be willing to assume management and clinical operations at West Suburban Medical Center."</p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: 14-year-old boy shot in the Loop
entries.summary: <p>A 14-year-old boy was shot in the Loop early Sunday, Chicago police said.</p><p>Officers found the boy suffering from a gunshot wound to the leg in the 100 block of East Wacker Drive around 3:30 a.m. The boy was "very uncooperative" and would not tell officers what happened, according to police.</p><p>He was taken in good condition to Lurie Children's Hospital. No one has been arrested, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Private services rescheduled for Chicago firefighter killed in Rogers Park blaze
entries.summary: <p>The private funeral for Michael Altman, the Chicago firefighter killed in a blaze earlier this month, was rescheduled for Tuesday.</p><p>The services, originally planned for last Friday, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/26/funeral-for-chicago-firefighter-killed-in-rogers-park-blaze-canceled-after-wife-goes-into-labor " target="_blank" >were canceled after Altman’s wife</a> went into labor Thursday. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/26/mourners-pay-respects-to-fallen-chicago-firefighter-michael-altman-its-beyond-words" target="_blank" >Visitation for Altman was still held Thursday</a> afternoon at St. Rita of Cascia Shrine Chapel, 7740 S. Western Ave. </p><p>The Altman family will now have invitation-only services at 10 a.m. Tuesday, March 31 at Blake-Lamb Funeral Home and Holy Sepulchre Catholic Cemetery, according to the Chicago Fire Department. </p><p>Anyone wishing to support the family Tuesday morning are invited to line the route from 103rd Street and Cicero Avenue to 111th Street to Holy Sepulchre, 6001 W. 111th St.</p><p>Altman <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/16/extra-alarm-blaze-erupts-at-a-rogers-park-building-possibly-injuring-firefighter" target="_blank" >died while fighting a fire</a> in a Rogers Park apartment March 16. He was among the crew who responded to the blaze in a four-story building in the 1700 block of West North Shore Avenue. He fell through a floor that collapsed.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: There is no debate over the ongoing abuse of immigrants
entries.summary: <p>Journalism is failing America.</p><p>At least mainstream media, newspapers and TV networks and such, the remnant still chugging away.</p><p>What's our job? To report the news, to convey what's happening.</p><p>And what do we do? Describe fantasies, ghosts, processes that aren't occurring.</p><p>Consider the recent murder of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman, out with a group of friends at a beach in Rogers Park when approached by a masked man, shot in the head and killed.</p><p>That was all too real.</p><p>But the moment the suspect was identified as a Venezuelan immigrant, the MAGA outrage machine started to whir. Led by President Donald Trump , who called him "an animal." The murder was waved about as confirmation. Because one member of a minority group always represents everybody in that group. While white folks, naturally, are individuals, each unique in his or her own way, hardly responsible for their own actions, never mind stand-ins for anybody else.</p><p>And how did the media present this deceptive carnival? This acrobatic leap from one crime on the North Side of Chicago, one victim and one suspect, to the entire country and all immigrants everywhere?</p><p>A conversation. An argument. The Sun-Times called it "a national immigration debate."</p><p>There is no conversation. No argument. Nobody is debating.</p><p>What is going on is dehumanization. The officially designated pariah group — nowadays, immigrants — is being stained with false rationales to justify their abuse: they are violent; they are diseased, they don't belong. If these rationals are contradictory — they are lazy and take all the good jobs — that doesn't matter. In 1930s, the Jews were both dirty vermin crouched in the shadows and jewel-encrusted millionaires secretly running the world. Few Germans seemed bothered by the contradiction.</p><p>The nation is busily building ... let's call them "detention camps" ... for this despised group .Billions are being spent ramping up a paramilitary force to snatch people off the street. Don't be fooled by the hesitation after Minneapolis. A temporary setback. The Democrats trying to get ICE under control is what the entire airport nightmare is about. Air travel in the United States is being throttled so we can deport more immigrants. A reminder that racism blows back on the racist. Trying to hurt others, they hurt themselves. Ask the Germans.</p><p>Why doesn't the media hit this point harder? Our error comes from a 7th grade civics class, how-a-bill-becomes-a-law mindset. It's our job to list the excuses given, noting, eventually, how inaccurate they are.</p><p>So we seek refuge recounting this Turning Point parody of debate. MAGA smugly states their premise: immigrants are violent murders who add nothing to this country and must be forcibly expelled now. They grin condescendingly while some garble is offered in reply — in actuality, immigrants are 2 to 4 times more law-abiding, as a group, than native-born U.S. citizens. Then they twist that reply: "So now you're slandering American citizens..."</p><p>And away we go.</p><p>Words don't mean anything because they're not listening. They don't care. As a Democrat, you're nearly subhuman already. Maybe you'll end up in those detention centers too. Gorman wasn't a person, in their eyes, she's a symbol, the latest in a list of sympathetic victims — always women — who can be performatively mourned while demanding faster deportations.</p><p>To argue is naive. It's like the SAVE Act, the supposed election security bill. Talk all you like a out the extreme rarity of voter fraud, they never reply, "Oh, right, point taken. Let's address actual problems." Because the bill isn't designed to stop the fraud that isn't there, it's designed to constrain voting — not to help voting, but hurt it. The war against immigrants isn't intended to stop crime; it's intended to commit crime, against people whose lack of legal status makes them easy targets. Hurting them is called patriotism.</p><p>The ongoing horror is getting to seem so ... ordinary. Business as usual.</p><p>"Man is a creature that can get used to anything," Dostoevsky writes in his semi-autobiographical novel, "The House of the Dead." </p><p>That gets quoted often. Less noted is its context. The observation comes after a description of the low, stuffy room that was the narrator's home for ten years, sleeping on boards, amidst "noise, uproar, laughter, swearing, the sound of chains, soot and fumes, shaven heads, branded faces, ragged clothes, all that is accursed and dishonored ... yes, man has great endurance."</p><p>Is this endurance a good thing? Maybe Americans allow themselves to endure far too much. Yes, a third No Kings protest was held Saturday. Important? Sure. Maybe. Or maybe protest, like the media observing its rituals, is part of making something normal that should never be considered normal. This isn't a debate. It's madness. And we know where madness leads, or should.</p>

entries.title: Basketball tournament makes space for trans athletes amid international sports bans
entries.summary: <p>Kienan Carpenter always dreamed of playing professional basketball.</p><p>When Carpenter, founder of the <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/the.tmasc/" target="_blank" >Trans Masculine Alliance Sports Club</a>, started transitioning at 22, he kept playing women’s basketball and flag football, and found inclusion, though he still faced misgendering, among other issues.</p><p>But on Sunday, teams of all genders and ages took to the courts for a three-versus-three basketball tournament held ahead of Tuesday's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/03/31/transgender-day-of-visibility-chicago-activists-city-hall" target="_blank" ><u>International Transgender Day of Visibility</u></a>.</p><p>It was the first of its kind for Carpenter and Mars Cantino, an organizer with <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/wnbgaychicago/" target="_blank" >WNBGAY</a>, who put the event together as a way to combat the exclusion they and other trans people have faced in sports.</p><p>“We shouldn’t have to sacrifice our identity to be included,” said Carpenter, 25. “If I can’t play professionally, how can I make a space that’s professional athletics for queer and trans people? So that’s the goal, there’s no limit, there’s no ‘I can’t’ — let me just make the space.”</p><p>The court, split into competitive and recreational sides, pitted players of all genders against each other for 15-minute matchups. Carpenter and Cantino first met three weeks ago and worked to coordinate their groups to make the tournament happen.</p><p>Supportive cheers filled the gaps between sneaker squeaks and the sound of the ball hitting the Center on Halsted’s hardwood gym floor.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gia drives to the hoop past Reene during a Transgender Day of Visibility 3x3 basketball tournament at Center on Halsted in Chicago on Sunday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Talia Sprague/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But it comes as trans people are further governed out of athletics.</p><p>As of Thursday, transgender women athletes are <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/ioc-olympic-transgender-female-eligibility-520cd9cee152a312767a667acf77dbc8" target="_blank" ><u>banned from women’s events at the Olympics</u></a> after the IOC agreed to a new eligibility policy that it says aligns with President Donald Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/02/keeping-men-out-of-womens-sports/" target="_blank" ><u>executive order</u></a> on sports ahead of the 2028 Los Angeles Games. Only one openly trans woman has ever competed in the Olympics: <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/2020-tokyo-olympics-sports-weightlifting-laurel-hubbard-e721827cdaf7299f47a9115a09c2a162" target="_blank" ><u>weightlifter Laurel Hubbard</u></a>, who left Tokyo in 2021 without a medal.</p><p>Trump issued an executive order last February threatening to withhold federal funding from schools that didn’t keep trans girls out of sports. His administration <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/maine-education-department-transgender-athletes-pam-bondi-5b5c8d0022233fae5ec60efc9fa5394f" target="_blank" ><u>sued Maine</u></a> for not complying.</p><p>He also backed an amendment to a voting bill that would’ve put that edict into the word of law, though the <a class="Link" href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2026/03/21/senate-trump-transgender-sports/" target="_blank" ><u>U.S. Senate rejected it last weekend</u></a>.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2026/03/29/northwestern-university-study-trans-gender-affirming-care-dysphoria-michael-bailey" >A Northwestern study claims to support trans youths’ wellbeing. Parents, advocates say it’ll do the opposite.</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/12/trans-former-employee-sues-cubs-over-discrimination" >Transgender former Cubs employee sues, alleges discrimination, silence on harassment</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/04/18/for-trans-athletes-in-high-schools-sports-ihsa-says-it-will-follow-state-law-not-trump-executive-order" >For trans athletes in high schools sports, IHSA says it will follow state law — not Trump executive order</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Breaking with the IOC — whose <a class="Link" href="https://stillmed.olympics.com/media/Documents/International-Olympic-Committee/IOC-Publications/EN-Olympic-Charter.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>Olympic Charter</u></a> states that access to play sport is a human right — Illinois has ignored the order, which isn’t legally binding, allowing its trans high school athletes <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/04/18/for-trans-athletes-in-high-schools-sports-ihsa-says-it-will-follow-state-law-not-trump-executive-order" target="_blank" ><u>to continue playing sports</u></a>. However, those young athletes themselves have been targeted by national groups descending on different localities to try to have them removed.</p><p>Last year, U.S. Cycling <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/cycling-female-transgender-athletes-ban-df45fe58ef1e4402c3996b57a0471df7" target="_blank" ><u>banned trans women athletes</u></a> who transitioned after going through a testosterone-based puberty and further restricted trans men competitors, just months after the first trans woman won one of its cycling events.</p><p>After that, Summer House pivoted from cycling to basketball, and while the Ravenswood resident was eventually allowed to play, she and other trans women faced pushback participating in the Chicago Metropolitan Sports League.</p><p>“[Overall], I thought organized sports was out the door,” House said.</p><p>But Sunday afternoon, House took to the court with the rest of the self-named Houston Rockettes, who donned green while her teammate, Kari Kinne, covered the boards with rebounds and blocks.</p><p>Kinne and House have been playing regularly in the WNBGay, a queer pickup basketball group started at Foster Beach four years ago that meets every Friday and Sunday for games.</p><p>That is was where Kinne felt most comfortable. She had played pickup games in high school and later at courts around the city, only to face hostility ranging from misgendering to harassment.</p><p>“Basketball was always one of those things I felt gatekept out of, even in high school,” said Kinne, 28. “I can go find a pickup game … and it’s mostly fine, but there’s always something, [like] getting called a slur. But I feel weird, out of my body a little bit.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" entries.title: One person dies after car crash on Eisenhower Expressway
entries.summary: <p>One person died after a three-car crash on the Eisenhower Expressway early Sunday, according to Illinois State Police.</p><p>The crash happened around 2:15 a.m. in the eastbound lanes on the I-290 at 1st Avenue, according to state police. One person was taken to an area hospital and later pronounced dead.</p><p>State police did not say how many passengers were in the three cars or their condition. The cause of the crash is under investigation. </p><p>The left three lanes were closed until just after 7 a.m. Sunday.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bulls need to prioritize what matters most and recent history shows why
entries.summary: <p>SAN ANTONIO – In the few impromptu conversations Arturas Karnisovas does have with members of the media throughout the season, he comes across as very normal.</p><p>The Bulls’ executive vice president of basketball operations has a dry sense of humor, is good for a laugh, and prioritizes family.</p><p>When it comes to his philosophy of roster construction and discussions surrounding that, however? Well, that’s where Karnisovas sounds more like a flat earther. The guy that is year after year denying the indisputable.</p><p>Thank goodness for Bulls guards Collin Sexton and Tre Jones tripping up at the horn in the final seconds of Saturday’s loss to Memphis or Sunday would feel a little different. The Bulls would be ahead of sinking Milwaukee in the standings, but behind them in the race for better draft lottery odds.</p><p>That means rather than having a 20.3% to hit top four, they would be down to 13.9%. Rather than hoping for the longshot of striking the No. 1 pick at 4.5%, it would be just 3%.</p><p>Not a big gap when one tries to math the math, but considering Karnisovas’ draft failures, he needs all the help he can get.</p><p>If staying the course can aid the Bulls and their front office to have an almost 7% chance to hit an AJ Dybantsa, Darryn Peterson, Cameron Boozer or Caleb Wilson, they should take it with a glance at the sky and a huge smile on their face.</p><p>In case Karnisovas & Co. still want to ignore that, maybe a reminder of the top of the NBA standings – territory very unfamiliar to them – will help.</p><p>Detroit and Boston sit atop the Eastern Conference, respectively, while defending champion Oklahoma City is trying to hold off San Antonio in the Western Conference. The four teams have a combined nine players on the roster that were drafted by them as top five picks.</p><p>The Celtics nailed it in back-to-back seasons with Jalen Brown (No. 3 in 2016) and Jayson Tatum (No. 3 in ’17), while the Pistons tanked their way to Cade Cunningham (No. 1) in 2021 and then followed that up with Ausar Thompson (No. 5 in ’23) and Ron Holland (No. 5 in ’24).</p><p>For the Spurs, it starts and ends with their understanding of knowing when to push all the chips into the center of the table and strike, landing No. 1 in the 2023 draft and getting Victor Wembanyama. The follow up to that was then Stephon Castle (No. 4 in ’24) and Dylan Harper (No. 2 in ’25), setting the stage for San Antonio to be a serious problem for the rest of the league for at least the next five years.</p><p>And don’t forget the Thunder, who have used draft assets and draft-class-talent-understanding to pick Chet Holmgren No. 2 overall (’22).</p><p>Even moving away from the top of the standings, if one needs an example of an up-and-coming team that has used the top of the lottery well the last five years take a look at Charlotte. The Hornets have three top 4 picks currently starting for them, going from a 19-win team last year to sitting at 39 entering Sunday.</p><p>What has Karnisovas put on his resume? One top five pick in six seasons and that was Patrick Williams at No. 4 in 2020.</p><p>Ignore the loud thud just heard in the background.</p><p>With just eight games left, including two games in lowly Washington in a few weeks, hopefully this will start to sink in for the Bulls’ executive. Josh Giddey has very little left to prove this season, while Sexton could very likely be elsewhere by July in free agency. It’s time to play the inexperienced guys and let nature take its course.</p><p>The NBA is a talent-acquisition based coloring book, and the top of the draft provides the best crayons. Karnisovas needs to start there.</p><p>Debating the earth being flat or a sphere? Save that for a later day.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Chase Meidroth 'locked in' atop White Sox' batting order
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> second baseman <b>Chase Meidroth</b>’s bat toss after his leadoff home run on Opening Day seemed to herald big things to start the year.</p><p>Unfortunately his teammates — aside from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/27/munetaka-murakami-home-run-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>slugging sensation</u></a> <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> — didn’t exactly follow suit in back-to-back slogs to start the year, but Meidroth hasn't let up in Milwaukee as the Sox' leadoff man cements himself in the organization’s plans.</p><p>The 24-year-old added a double Saturday to go 3-for-6 with two walks over the first two games while bringing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/06/09/white-sox-chase-meidroth-edge-animal-all-star-game-grady-sizemore-josh-rojas" target="_blank" ><u>an edge to the top of the order</u></a> along with a welcome dose of sound defense. He made a diving catch in the first inning of Sunday's game. </p><p>“Just finding my rhythm again,” said Meidroth, whose .253/.329/.320 rookie campaign was hampered by a nagging hand injury.</p><p>“He’s just locked in at the plate,” manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. “We know he treats every pitch like it’s his last and wants to make the right decision up there. He’s just extremely competitive, [has] put himself in a really nice spot at the top of the order to really set the tone for our group.”</p><p>“We’re seeing the best version of him right now,” Venable added.</p><h3><b>Greener pastures</b></h3><p>Sox castoff <b>Luis Robert Jr.</b> introduced himself to Mets with a bang, slamming a walk-off three-run homer in his second game in Queens on Saturday.</p><p>The five-tool talent who never quite found his groove in Chicago went 3-for-8 with five RBI and two walks in his first two games with New York, after Sox GM <b>Chris Getz</b> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/01/20/white-sox-trade-luis-robert-jr-to-the-mets" target="_blank" ><u>dealt him</u></a> in exchange for utility player <b>Luisangel Acuña</b>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">WELCOME TO THE NEW YORK METS, LUIS!!! <a href="https://t.co/kSJOou0hhu">pic.twitter.com/kSJOou0hhu</a></p>— New York Mets (@Mets) <a href="https://twitter.com/Mets/status/2038036991191777306?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 28, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>His splashy Big Apple debut was salt in the wound for Sox fans, whom Robert jabbed before Opening Day when he correctly predicted to reporters that Citi Field was “going to be packed, which is something that over the last few years in Chicago I didn’t get to experience.”</p><p>Time will tell if Robert finds sustained success like <b>Andrew Vaughn</b> found last year after being dumped by the Sox and scooped up by the Brewers <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2025/10/09/the-game-humbled-him-how-former-white-sox-first-baseman-andrew-vaughn-rejuvenated-his-career-with-brewers" target="_blank" ><u>en route to their NLCS run</u></a>.</p><p>Vaughn, who hit .308/.375/.493 in Milwaukee after extended struggles on the South Side, knocked an RBI single off the Sox Thursday in his first appearance against the team that drafted him. But he also suffered a hand injury that’s expected to sideline him till May.</p><p>“Another baseball game with a team on the other side,” Vaughn said before missing the final two games of the series against his old squad. “Can’t really look at it any other way.”</p><h3><b>That’s a keeper</b></h3><p>Center fielder <b>Tristan Peters</b>, an offseason acquisition from the Rays, talked his way into his first career hit Saturday.</p><p>The 26-year-old Manitoba native roped a liner to center in the fourth inning on a 3-2 pitch from Brewers starter <b>Chad Patrick</b>, and "the whole way I was saying, 'Just get down, get down, get down,' but I didn't think so at first,” Peters said.</p><p>But a diving <b>Garrett Mitchell </b>couldn’t haul it in, resulting in a double on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/28/white-sox-prospects-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci-triple-a-opening-day" target="_blank" ><u>an otherwise moribund evening</u></a> for Sox hitters.</p><p>Peters, who played at Southern Illinois University, went hitless in his first four MLB games last year at Tampa Bay. Getting it out of the way was “huge” for the outfielder, who’s got an early-season platoon role.</p><p>“I got an opportunity last year and wasn't able to get my hit,” he said. “It kinda plays with you mentally a bit. You start to press, kind of throw away at-bats, but I was trying not to press too much, just kind of do my thing."</p><p><i>Contributing: Steve Greenberg</i><br></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Colson Montgomery’s grand slam, another Murakami homer can’t save White Sox from bullpen meltdown
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — It looked like a Colson Montgomery grand slam and yet another home run from Munetaka Murakami on Sunday might cure what had ailed the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> on a nauseating opening weekend.</p><p>But a revamped Sox bullpen showed the same leaks it has for years as a six-run eighth-inning meltdown gave way to a deflating 9-7 loss to the Brewers, capped by Christian Yelich’s go-ahead three-run blast off newly signed Sox closer Seranthony Dominguez.</p><p>It all but snuffed out any positive takeaways for the Sox, who led by five runs but still got swept by last year’s NLCS contenders.</p><p>“Certainly some things that we have to work on,” manager Will Venable said of his squad’s ugly first series. “Some of the things that we emphasized in spring training that we got exposed with, to be honest.”</p><p>The Sox were shoddy in all phases in their first two blowout losses, but their bats came alive early in the finale.</p><p>Montgomery launched the second grand slam of his young career after the Sox flooded the bases to lead off the game with walks drawn by second baseman Chase Meidroth and Murakami, followed by third baseman Miguel Vargas’ bloop single.</p><p>There was no doubt about the 104-mph blast Montgomery launched 405 feet over the center field wall for his first homer of the season after a whacking 21 his rookie year.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Colson Montgomery GRAND SLAM 😤 <a href="https://t.co/YOwPC887Il">pic.twitter.com/YOwPC887Il</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2038321165987381514?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 29, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Milwaukee bounced back against Sox starter Anthony Kay with a two-run homer from Gary Sanchez in the bottom of the inning, but Murakami responded with his third dinger in his third career game, glancing off Milwaukee right fielder Sal Frelick’s glove to escape American Family Field.</p><p>The usually stoic Murakami pumped a fist as he rounded the bases, becoming just the fourth player in MLB history <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/27/munetaka-murakami-home-run-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>to go yard</u></a> in each of his first three career games.</p><p>“I’m truly grateful and happy that I was able to keep that kind of record, but like I said before, there’s still a long way to go and a lot of ways to improve,” Murakami said through a translator.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">THREE MLB GAMES <br>THREE HOME RUNS <br><br>Munetaka Murakami is blasting baseballs to start his MLB career 😤 <a href="https://t.co/Gnprk793bO">pic.twitter.com/Gnprk793bO</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2038327263196770467?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 29, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Montgomery tabbed his fifth RBI of the game later that inning with a base knock to score Vargas, who had singled and stolen second on a brutal afternoon for Brewers starter Brandon Sproat.</p><p>Sox right fielder Everson Pereira, who had a forgettable first two games, spanked one 403 feet the next inning off Sproat, who was rocked for seven runs on four walks and six hits.</p><p>Kay bent but didn’t break in 4 ⅔ innings of two-run ball, giving up three hits and four walks to go with five strikeouts in his Sox debut after signing a two-year, $12 million deal <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/12/09/anthony-kay-regained-his-confidence-overseas-white-sox-hope-he-can-translate-that-success-to-the-majors" target="_blank" ><u>following a stint in Japan</u></a>.</p><p>Offseason arrival Chris Murphy dug the Sox’ eighth inning hole and left a one-out, bases-loaded jam for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/01/30/seranthony-dominguez-to-stabilize-white-sox-bullpen-late-in-games" target="_blank" ><u>closer Dominguez</u></a>, who drew a popout and was a strike away from escaping, till Luis Rengifo dribbled a two-run single up the middle. Then Yelich delivered the dagger with a 421-foot, pinch-hit bomb to the upper deck.</p><p>“I was so close to getting out of that inning and just made a big mistake right down the middle,” Dominguez said.</p><p>Outscored <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/28/white-sox-prospects-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci-triple-a-opening-day" target="_blank" ><u>29-10 in their first series</u></a>, a deflated Sox squad heads to Miami in search of their first win — and any hint that this rebuild can take a serious step forward in 2026.</p><p>“You are going to take your lumps like any team does,” general manager Chris Getz said before the Sox took three big ones to start the year. “I do think that as the season progresses, much like the second half last year, there will be stretches where we are really starting to be competitive on a regular basis and winning series against clubs that are at the top of the division or the top of the league.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Illinois, Michigan give Big Ten two shots at ending quarter-century-long title drought. Is this the year?
entries.summary: <p>For only the fourth time in the 2000s, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Big Ten</a> has two teams — Midwest Region No. 1 seed Michigan and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/28/illinois-iowa-final-four-wagler-stirtz-underwood-mccollum-ncaa-tournament-elite-eight-march-madness" target="_blank" >South Region No. 3 seed Illinois</a> — in the Final Four of the men’s NCAA Tournament.</p><p>Maybe this time, the conference’s quarter-century-and-counting championship drought will actually come to an end.</p><p>Michigan and West No. 1 Arizona will clash amid game-of-the-year hype in Indianapolis. These have been the best two teams in this tournament, hands down. Not that the semifinal matchup of Illinois and East No. 2 UConn will be lacking a thing.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/28/illinois-iowa-final-four-wagler-stirtz-underwood-mccollum-ncaa-tournament-elite-eight-march-madness" >Illinois Final Four-bound for first time since 2005 after beating Iowa in South Region final</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2025/08/29/illinois-basketball-2005-dee-brown-deron-williams-luther-head-bruce-weber-north-carolina" target="_blank" >Illinois basketball greats of 2005 gather for sweet reunion, championship regrets aside</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>So, what happened the last three times the Big Ten sent two of its finest onto the grandest stage in college basketball?</p><p><b>2000: 1 Michigan State and 8 Wisconsin</b></p><p>The Big Ten was guaranteed to have a team in the final, because the Spartans and Badgers collided in the semis. How ugly was it? The Spartans went over 11 minutes without a bucket, yet they won easily as the Badgers mustered all of 41 points. Don’t you miss the Dick Bennett days? Season series: 4-0 in favor of the Big Green.</p><p>The Spartans cut down the nets in Indianapolis after beating Florida 89-76 two nights later. Star Mateen Cleaves climbed the ladder gingerly, sprained ankle and all. Tom Izzo’s hair? Black. This remains the league’s last title.</p><p><b>2005: 1 Illinois and 5 Michigan State</b></p><p>Merciless North Carolina dominated the Spartans 87-71, then <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2025/08/29/illinois-basketball-2005-dee-brown-deron-williams-luther-head-bruce-weber-north-carolina" target="_blank" >nipped the Illini 75-70</a> in the final. It was an anticlimactic end to an amazing season powered by Dee Brown, Deron Williams and Luther Head, but the Tar Heels were just a bit better in St. Louis. Too much — make that way too much — Sean May.</p><p>“We went down fighting,” Bruce Weber said. “I can’t ask for more.”</p><p><b>2015: 1 Wisconsin, 7 Michigan State</b></p><p>The Badgers were back in the Final Four for the second straight year and upset unbeaten Kentucky 71-64 in the semis, maybe the greatest night ever for the cheddarheads. But Duke, after blasting the Spartans 81-61, roared back from nine down in the second half of a brutally physical final to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2015/4/6/18588836/freshmen-lead-duke-past-wisconsin-for-national-title" target="_blank" >break the Badgers’ hearts 68-63</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/zAT_O_TJais?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="North Carolina vs. Illinois: Final 5 minutes of 2005 National Championship"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: What to expect from the Bears at the NFL's Annual Meeting
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The Bears followed their most promising season in a generation with an offseason that’s produced more questions than answers. The Bears haven’t replaced some of their most prominent players and still don’t know what city — or state — will become the home of their new football stadium.</p><p>The Bears’ decision-makers have gathered at the NFL Annual Meetings, where they’ll try to make progess on those issues — and more — through Wednesday.<br>Here’s a look at what to watch for:</p><h3>Sweet home, Hammond?</h3><p>At the annual meetings a year ago, the Bears pivoted their public stadium strategy. President/CEO Kevin Warren said then that the Bears were no longer limited themselves to building on the Lakefront and instead would consider — for a second time — building on the Arlington Heights site they own.</p><p>A year later, the Bears still don‘t know where they’ll build their next stadium. Wednesday, Warren and chairman George McCaskey will be forced to, for the first time, answer questions about the stadium deal offered them by the state of Indiana earlier this month. If the Bears take it, they’ll build in Hammond.</p><p>The Bears haven’t given Indiana a commitment, though, and continue to push for legislation to shrink their property taxes in Arlington Heights. The Bears consider the next two months to be critical to their Illinois stadium push — the Illinois General Assembly has until May to pass a so-called mega-projects bill.</p><h3>Feeling a draft</h3><p>General manager Ryan Poles landed in the Valley of the Sun early, attending Arizona State’s pro day Friday. The Sun Devils boast Max Iheanachor, a tackle with otherworldly athleticism who, amazingly, didn’t play football until junior college. ASU receiver Jordyn Tyson is another potential first-round pick.</p><p>With less than a month to go before the draft, Poles will have to answer Tuesday how much pressure he feels to fill the gaps the Bears now have at edge rusher, defensive tackle, safety offensive tackle and cornerback — likely, in that order. The Bears have three picks in the top 60 and four in the top 89, though Poles is notorious for trading down in the draft.</p><h3>Ben explains</h3><p>The Bears’ flurry of cuts, trades and free agent additions earlier this month were justified earlier this month by Poles — but not coach Ben Johnson. Monday, Johnson will meet the media for the first time since days after the Bears’ season-ending loss.</p><p>Johnson, one of the most respected offensive minds in the league who has increasingly gained power in the Bears’ decision-making process, will have to answer for his role in the team’s decision to trade receiver DJ Moore, cut Tremaine Edmunds and let safety Kevin Byard leave via free agency. Looking ahead, Johnson will have to answer whether he believes the Bears have done enough to help Caleb Williams take the next step — they replaced Moore and retired center Drew Dalman with modest additions.</p><h3>Goodell, too</h3><p>Commissioner Roger Goodell will address pressing league issues Tuesday, including the state of Florida’s claim that the Rooney Rule might violate state law. Adjacent to the league’s requirement to interview minority candidates for upper-tier jobs is the rule that miffed the Bears, who believed they were due two supplemental third-round picks when they lost assistant general manager Ian Cunningham to the Falcons’ GM job. The league ruled, however, that Falcons president of football Matt Ryan was the team’s chief-decision maker, costing the Bears the picks.</p><p>Goodell will also address the league’s international push, of which the Bears could be a part — the Falcons still haven’t announced their Madrid opponent — and the ongoing negotiations with the NFL’s referee union.</p><p>Rule changes up for consideration this week would allow the NFL to use replay to fix a call made by replacement officials in case of a strike. Another proposal would allow teams to boot an onside kick at any point during the game. The legality of the “Tush Push,” for once, is not up for debate.<br></p>
